content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 202–203 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_005_page_001.jpg, DEU_005_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 147–148

---

1 And Moses summoned all Israel and said to them: "Hear, O Israel, the statutes and the judgments that I speak in your ears today, and you shall learn them and take care to do them.

2 The LORD our God made a covenant with us at Horeb.

3 Not with our fathers did the LORD make this covenant, but with us — we, all of us who are alive here today.

4 Face to face the LORD spoke with you at the mountain from the midst of the fire,

5 while I stood between the LORD and you at that time, to declare to you the word of the LORD — for you were afraid because of the fire and did not go up the mountain — saying:

6 I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

7 You shall have no other gods before me.

8 You shall not make for yourself a carved image — any likeness of what is in the heavens above, or what is on the earth below, or what is in the water under the earth.

9 You shall not bow down to them or serve them; for I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children, even upon the third and fourth generation of those who hate me,

10 but showing covenant-faithfulness to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.

11 You shall not take up the name of the LORD your God in a vain and destructive way, for the LORD will not hold guiltless the one who takes up his name in a vain and destructive way.

12 Observe the Sabbath day to keep it holy, as the LORD your God commanded you.

13 Six days you shall labor and do all your work,

14 but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God. You shall not do any work — you, or your son or your daughter, or your male slave or your female slave, or your ox or your donkey or any of your livestock, or the sojourner who is within your gates — so that your male slave and your female slave may rest as well as you.

15 You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God brought you out from there with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm. Therefore the LORD your God commanded you to keep the Sabbath day.

16 Honor your father and your mother, as the LORD your God commanded you, so that your days may be long and that it may go well with you in the land that the LORD your God is giving you.

17 You shall not murder.

18 And you shall not commit adultery.

19 And you shall not steal.

20 And you shall not testify against your neighbor with a false witness.

21 And you shall not covet your neighbor's wife. And you shall not desire your neighbor's house, his field, or his male slave, or his female slave, his ox or his donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor.

22 These words the LORD spoke to your entire assembly at the mountain from the midst of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness — a great voice — and he added nothing more. And he wrote them on two tablets of stone and gave them to me.

23 And it came to pass when you heard the voice from the midst of the darkness, while the mountain was burning with fire, that you drew near to me — all the heads of your tribes and your elders —

24 and you said: Behold, the LORD our God has shown us his glory and his greatness, and we have heard his voice from the midst of the fire. This day we have seen that God speaks with man and man still lives.

25 And now, why should we die? For this great fire will consume us. If we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, we shall die.

26 For who is there of all flesh that has heard the voice of the living God speaking from the midst of the fire as we have, and lived?

27 You go near and hear all that the LORD our God will say, and you speak to us all that the LORD our God will speak to you, and we will hear and do it.

28 And the LORD heard the voice of your words when you spoke to me, and the LORD said to me: I have heard the voice of the words of this people that they have spoken to you. All that they have spoken is good.

29 Oh, that their heart were like this — to fear me and to keep all my commandments always — so that it might go well with them and with their children forever!

30 Go and say to them: Return to your tents.

31 But you, stand here with me, and I will speak to you all the commandments and the statutes and the judgments that you shall teach them, so that they may do them in the land that I am giving them to possess.

32 So you shall take care to do as the LORD your God has commanded you. You shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left.

33 You shall walk in all the way that the LORD your God has commanded you, so that you may live and that it may go well with you, and that you may prolong your days in the land that you are to possess.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**"Face to face" (v.4, panim be-fanim):** Literally "face in face" — an idiom of direct, immediate encounter. LOGOS preserves the phrase. The LXX: prosōpon kata prosōpon — "face against face." The striking claim that Israel encountered God "face to face" at Sinai is immediately qualified by v.5: Moses mediated because Israel feared. The tension is deliberate and LOGOS does not smooth it.

**"Not with our fathers — but with us" (v.3):** A theologically charged statement of contemporaneity. Every generation stands at Sinai. LOGOS preserves the emphatic "us, all of us who are alive here today" (itanu anachnu elleh poh ha-yom kullanu chayyim). The LXX renders the emphasis with hēmin autois — "with us ourselves."

**"House of slavery" (v.6, beit avadim):** The standard Deuteronomic epithet for Egypt. LOGOS maintains "house of slavery" consistently with Deuteronomy 4 and elsewhere. The LXX: ex oikou douleias.

**"Covenant-faithfulness" for hesed (v.10):** LOGOS follows the established project convention: hesed = "covenant-faithfulness" (set in the Genesis translation conventions). The LXX: eleos — "mercy," which is narrower. "Thousands" (la-alaphim) is deliberately indefinite in comparison with the finite "third and fourth generation" of divine punishment — the asymmetry is intentional.

**"You shall not take up the name of the LORD your God in a vain and destructive way" (v.11):** The Hebrew lo tissa et-shem YHWH elohekha la-shav. The word shav means emptiness, vanity, nothingness — but also falsehood and destruction. LOGOS renders "in a vain and destructive way" to capture both dimensions. The LXX: epi mataiō — "for a vain/empty purpose." Most translations simplify to "misuse" or "take in vain," losing the texture of the term.

**"Observe" vs. "Remember" the Sabbath (v.12):** Deuteronomy 5 uses shamor (observe/guard) while the Exodus 20 version uses zakhor (remember). LOGOS renders shamor as "observe" to preserve the distinction. The LXX of Deuteronomy 5 has phylaxē — "guard." This difference between the two Decalogue versions is one of the most textually significant divergences in the Pentateuch.

**Reason for Sabbath (v.15):** Deuteronomy grounds the Sabbath in the Exodus from Egypt (remember you were slaves) rather than in creation rest (Exod 20:11). LOGOS does not harmonize. This theological difference between the two versions is preserved exactly as the text reads.

**"You shall not murder" (v.17, lo tirtsach):** The Hebrew root ratsach refers specifically to unauthorized killing — murder — not all killing (killing in war or capital punishment use other verbs). LOGOS renders "murder." The LXX: ou phoneuseis — "you shall not kill." The LXX is ambiguous; the Hebrew is more precise.

**"You shall not covet... you shall not desire" (v.21):** Two distinct verbs in Deuteronomy 5: lo tachmod (covet) for the wife, lo tit'aveh (desire/crave) for the house and property. Exodus 20:17 uses lo tachmod for both. LOGOS preserves Deuteronomy's two-verb version. The LXX Deuteronomy: ouk epithumēseis... ouk epithumēseis (same verb twice — flattening the Masoretic distinction).

**"A great voice — and he added nothing more" (v.22):** The phrase we-lo yasaf (literally "and he did not add") is one of the most debated in the Decalogue. LOGOS renders the ambiguous "he added nothing more" — meaning either (a) God did not add to what was spoken, or (b) the voice was like no other. The LXX: kai ou prosethēken — "and he did not add."

**"Oh, that their heart were like this" (v.29):** A divine lament — mi yiten (literally "who will give") expressing a wish. LOGOS renders it as an optative exclamation. The LXX: tis dōsei — "who will give." The divine longing for Israel's faithfulness is among the most affectively powerful moments of Moses' address.

*Translation by LOGOS | Deuteronomy Chapter 5 | LOGOS Bible (Formal Equivalence)*
